bibliographic_data.title: "It Feels Like Taking a Gamble": Exploring Perceptions, Practices, and Challenges of Using Makeup and Cosmetics for People with Visual Impairments
bibliographic_data.abstract: For the estimated 44% of the US population that regularly uses cosmetic products [53], a makeup practice constitutes one of the most important avenues toward self-expression and self-care. Despite this fact, there is a near-absent representation of makeup use by people with visual impairments, a worldwide population of at least 2.2 billion [96]. Makeup practices are equally prevalent and meaningful for people with visual impairments, and embedded in a rich constellation of culture and identity signifiers [66]. The paper presents a content analysis of 145 YouTube videos to demonstrate visually impaired individuals’ unique practices before, during, and after doing makeup. Based on the makeup practices, semi-structured interviews were conducted with 12 visually impaired people to discuss their perceptions of and challenges with the makeup process in more depth. Overall, through our findings and discussion, we present novel perceptions of makeup from visually impaired individuals (e.g., broader representations of blindness and beauty). The existing challenges provide opportunities for future research to address learning barriers, insufficient feedback, and physical and environmental barriers, making the experience of doing makeup more accessible to people with visual impairments.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper presents novel perceptions of makeup from visually impaired individuals and identifies existing challenges that can be addressed through future research in assistive technology.
core_content.key_methodology: A content analysis of 145 YouTube videos was conducted, followed by semi-structured interviews with 12 visually impaired people to explore their practices, perceptions, and challenges related to makeup.
outcomes: Novel perceptions of makeup from visually impaired individuals
Identification of existing challenges in the makeup process for visually impaired individuals
core_content.full_text_markdown:
# Introduction
For the estimated 44% of the US population that regularly uses cosmetic products [53], a makeup practice constitutes one of the most important avenues toward self-expression and self-care. Despite this fact, there is a near-absent representation of makeup use by people with visual impairments, a worldwide population of at least 2.2 billion [96]. Makeup practices are equally prevalent and meaningful for people with visual impairments, and embedded in a rich constellation of culture and identity signifiers [66]. The paper presents a content analysis of 145 YouTube videos to demonstrate visually impaired individuals’ unique practices before, during, and after doing makeup. Based on the makeup practices, semi-structured interviews were conducted with 12 visually impaired people to discuss their perceptions of and challenges with the makeup process in more depth. Overall, through our findings and discussion, we present novel perceptions of makeup from visually impaired individuals (e.g., broader representations of blindness and beauty). The existing challenges provide opportunities for future research to address learning barriers, insufficient feedback, and physical and environmental barriers, making the experience of doing makeup more accessible to people with visual impairments.
